title: Argo CD 3.5 Tightens Supply Chain Security with Internal mTLS and Source Integrity
gist: Argo CD 3.5 (released June 2026) adds mutual TLS enforcement for internal component communication, Git commit signature verification for supply chain security, and a native ApplicationSet UI — closing longstanding gaps relative to Flux v2 while graduating impersonation and Source Hydrator from alpha to beta.

themes.theme: Internal mTLS Enforcement Closes Security Gap

insights.claim: Prior to Argo CD 3.5, communication between the repo-server and other internal components such as the API server and controllers was entirely unencrypted, leaving internal traffic outside the mTLS protection typically applied at the ingress layer.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: This historical gap means enterprises running large-scale Argo CD deployments before v3.5 had unencrypted east-west traffic that could be intercepted or tampered with, a material security risk for regulated industries.
insights.quote: Communication between the repo-server and other components, like the API server and controllers, was unencrypted before.

insights.claim: Argo CD 3.5 introduces mTLS for the repo-server, requiring client certificates from each connecting component; for environments without custom certificates, the server creates self-signed certs in memory to avoid filesystem dependencies.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: The in-memory self-signed cert fallback lowers the barrier to adoption, enabling teams without a PKI to gain internal mTLS coverage immediately on upgrade.
insights.quote: For environments without custom certificates, the repo-server creates self-signed certs in memory. This helps with internal health checks and avoids relying on the filesystem.

themes.theme: Supply Chain Security via Git Commit Signature Verification

insights.claim: Before Argo CD 3.5, nothing in Argo CD prevented a compromised Git repository from silently deploying unsigned or tampered manifests to Kubernetes clusters.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Supply chain attacks targeting GitOps pipelines had a viable path through Argo CD prior to this release — a systemic risk for organizations deploying with Argo CD at scale.
insights.quote: nothing in Argo CD prevented a compromised Git repository from silently deploying unsigned or tampered manifests.

insights.claim: Argo CD 3.5 Source Integrity validation lets operators enforce Git commit signatures before syncing via sourceIntegrity.required: true in the Application spec or the CLI flag --source-integrity-required.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Operators can now enforce cryptographically verified deployments natively within Argo CD without relying on external admission webhooks or Git provider policies.
insights.quote: Source Integrity validation lets operators ensure Git sources have valid signatures before syncing. This can be set up through the Application spec with sourceIntegrity.required: true

insights.claim: Flux v2 has offered native GPG commit signature verification via spec.verify.mode: head in the GitRepository API longer than Argo CD, meaning Argo CD 3.5 is catching up to Flux on this security feature.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Teams evaluating GitOps tools on security posture should note that Flux had a head start on supply chain verification; Argo CD 3.5 narrows but may not eliminate that gap depending on implementation depth.
insights.quote: Flux has offered native GPG support in the GitRepository API via spec.verify.mode: head longer than Argo CD, so Argo CD is catching up here.

insights.claim: Argo CD 3.5 introduces a native ApplicationSet UI — built by engineers from Intuit, Red Hat, GoTo, and Octopus Deploy — with list, filter, detail views, and a Preview Apps tab that shows what applications an ApplicationSet template will generate before deployment.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: The Preview Apps tab directly reduces misconfiguration risk in large-scale GitOps deployments by letting operators validate ApplicationSet outputs before they are applied, closing an operational visibility gap.
insights.quote: The ApplicationSet UI, created by engineers from Intuit, Red Hat, GoTo, and Octopus Deploy, includes list, filter, and detail views. It also has a Preview Apps tab, which shows the applications an ApplicationSet template will create before deployment.

insights.claim: Argo CD 3.5 allows ApplicationSets to be deployed in any namespace rather than only the Argo CD namespace, meeting a long-requested requirement for teams managing GitOps by namespace.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Multi-tenant Kubernetes platform teams can now delegate ApplicationSet management per namespace, reducing centralized bottlenecks and improving namespace-scoped self-service.
insights.quote: ApplicationSets can now be deployed in any namespace, not just the Argo CD namespace. This change brings stable status and meets a long-requested need for teams managing GitOps by namespace.

insights.claim: ApplicationSet concurrency controls in Argo CD 3.5 allow operators to cap the number of applications processed simultaneously, reducing the risk of overwhelming the cluster or upstream Git APIs during large sync operations.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: For organizations with hundreds or thousands of applications managed via ApplicationSet, uncontrolled concurrency has been a reliability risk; this control improves operational stability.
insights.quote: ApplicationSet concurrency controls help operators limit the number of applications processed at the same time. This reduces the risk of overwhelming the cluster or upstream Git APIs.

insights.claim: Argo CD 3.5 graduates impersonation to beta, enabling Argo CD to assume a specific user identity for server-side operations (log streaming, resource deletion, sync), which is critical for maintaining accurate audit trails in multi-tenant Kubernetes clusters.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Compliance-sensitive organizations using Argo CD in multi-tenant environments gain a production-ready mechanism to attribute cluster operations to specific identities rather than a shared service account.
insights.quote: When you set up impersonation through AppProject or RBAC policy, it now applies automatically to all server operations. This is important for audit trails in multi-tenant clusters.

themes.theme: Source Hydrator and Multi-Repository GitOps Patterns

insights.claim: Argo CD 3.5 graduates Source Hydrator to beta, enabling teams to configure separate repository URLs for dry (unhydrated) source templates and synced (hydrated) manifests, supporting multi-repository GitOps workflows with independent access controls per repo.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Platform teams can now enforce separation of concerns between the source of truth (template repo) and the deployed state (manifest repo), enabling fine-grained RBAC and audit at each layer.
insights.quote: Teams can now set different drySource.repoURL and syncSource.repoURL values. This enables multi-repository GitOps patterns. Here, source templates and rendered manifests are in different repositories.

insights.claim: Argo CD 3.5 addresses Azure AD group claims overflow by routing group resolution through the Microsoft Graph API, bypassing OIDC token size limits that caused authentication failures for large enterprises with many Azure AD groups.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Large Azure AD organizations that previously hit OIDC token size ceilings with Argo CD can now reliably use group-based RBAC without workarounds.
insights.quote: Azure AD users hitting group claims overflow can now route group resolution through the Microsoft Graph API, bypassing OIDC token size limits.

insights.claim: Argo CD 3.5 adds Service Principal authentication for Azure DevOps repositories, eliminating the dependency on Personal Access Tokens (PATs) which are tied to individual user accounts and present a rotation and security management burden.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Enterprises using Azure DevOps for GitOps pipelines gain a more secure and auditable authentication path that isn't invalidated by employee departures or PAT expiry.
insights.quote: Azure DevOps repositories gain Service Principal authentication support, eliminating the PAT dependency.

themes.theme: Competitive Positioning vs. Flux and Rancher Fleet

insights.claim: Flux v2 avoids the internal mTLS security problem by design — its controllers communicate via Kubernetes API objects rather than direct gRPC, meaning there is no internal unencrypted traffic to secure, a fundamental architectural advantage over Argo CD's historical approach.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Argo CD's internal mTLS addition is remediation of a past architectural debt; Flux's design avoided this class of vulnerability entirely, which matters for security-first tooling evaluations.
insights.quote: Flux (v2.8, March 2026) avoids the internal mTLS issue by design. Its controllers use Kubernetes API objects to communicate instead of direct gRPC. This means there's no internal traffic that needs securing.

insights.claim: Rancher Fleet lacks a built-in Source Integrity mechanism; enforcing commit signatures requires external policy from the Git provider or an admission webhook, making it less self-contained than Argo CD 3.5 for supply chain security.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Organizations evaluating GitOps tools specifically for supply chain security will find Argo CD 3.5 and Flux both ahead of Rancher Fleet, which delegates this concern entirely to external tooling.
insights.quote: Fleet lacks a built-in Source Integrity mechanism. To enforce commit signatures, you'll need a policy from your Git provider or an admission webhook.

insights.claim: Jenkins X enforces supply chain controls at the pipeline layer via Tekton Chains and GPG-signed releases rather than at the GitOps sync layer, and lacks a first-party dashboard or ApplicationSet-equivalent preview capability.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: Jenkins X occupies a different architectural niche (pipeline-centric) compared to Argo CD and Flux (sync-centric), and is less comparable for teams evaluating dedicated GitOps controllers.
insights.quote: Jenkins X uses Tekton to enforce supply chain controls at the pipeline layer. This includes Tekton Chains and GPG-signed releases. It does not provide a first-party dashboard and lacks a preview capability like ApplicationSet.

insights.claim: Flux 2.8 added a web dashboard via the separately-installed Flux Operator covering Kustomization and HelmRelease views, but lacks an ApplicationSet Preview equivalent, giving Argo CD 3.5 a UI differentiation advantage for visual pre-deployment validation.
insights.speaker: Claudio Masolo
insights.so_what: For platform teams that value visual operational tooling, Argo CD 3.5's Preview Apps tab is a meaningful differentiator that Flux has not yet matched.
insights.quote: Flux 2.8 added a web dashboard to the UI via the separate Flux Operator. This includes views for Kustomization and HelmRelease. However, it lacks an ApplicationSet Preview equivalent.
